title: Roast potatoes
subtitle: Parboiled, roughed up, roasted in hot fat
excerpt: Floury potatoes parboiled until the edges start to soften, drained and shaken to rough up the surface, then roasted in very hot fat until crisp and golden. The three-stage method (parboil, shake, roast) is the standard approach. Goose fat produces the crispest result; vegetable oil is reliable.
sourceNotes: Roast potatoes are a non-negotiable part of the British Sunday roast. The debate about which fat and which potato has been ongoing for decades. Maris Piper and King Edward are the standard floury varieties for the job. Goose fat is the Christmas standard; beef dripping produces a different but equally good result.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Parboil and shake the day before, spread on a tray, and refrigerate uncovered overnight. The dried-out surface produces an even crispier result.

content.text: The parboil must go far enough: the edges of the potato should be starting to soften and break apart before draining. Under-parboiling means the surface stays smooth and does not rough up when shaken. The rough, starchy edges are what become the crispy layer in the oven.

The fat must be smoking hot before the potatoes go in. A cold-fat start produces soft roasties, not crispy ones. The potatoes must also be well-drained and steam-dried before hitting the fat — any surface moisture will lower the temperature of the fat and produce steam.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into large chunks
or goose fat, duck fat, or beef dripping
for the water and for finishing

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C. Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} into a large roasting tin and place in the oven. Put the {{potato}} in a pan, cover with cold salted water, and bring to the boil. Boil for 10 minutes, until the edges are beginning to soften. Drain thoroughly. Return the potatoes to the pan, cover with the lid, and shake vigorously for 5 to 10 seconds to rough up the surface. Remove the hot roasting tin from the oven. Carefully tip the potatoes into the hot fat, turning to coat. The fat should sizzle and spit immediately. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}}. Roast for 45 to 50 minutes, turning halfway through, until deeply golden and crisp on all sides. Drain on kitchen paper for 2 minutes if needed, season again, and serve.

items.symptom: Soft on the outside, not crispy
items.cause: Fat not hot enough, potatoes too wet, or oven temperature too low
items.fix: The fat must be smoking before the potatoes go in. Steam-dry the potatoes for 2 minutes after draining. Do not overcrowd the tin — use two tins if needed

items.symptom: Sticking to the tin when you try to turn them
items.fix: If they stick when you try to turn them, leave them another 5 minutes. When they are properly crisped they will release from the tin without tearing

items.cause: Oven too low, or tin too deep trapping steam
items.fix: Increase to 230°C and give them another 10 minutes. Shallow tins allow moisture to escape; deep tins trap steam and produce a paler result

content.text: Roast potatoes are one of the most argued-about elements of British Sunday cooking. Fat choice, potato variety, and the precise degree of parboiling are all subjects of strong opinion. The method above produces a reliable result; fat and variety are adjustable without affecting the technique.
